quality.rationale: Abstract tensor product condition verified via matrix proportionality and coordinate-free directional mapping; complete logical derivation but lacks concrete physical application scenario.

origin.question:
Problem 3.11. You are given a tensor $\mathbf{K}^{\alpha  eta}$ . How can you test whether it is a direct product of two vectors $\mathbf{K}^{\alpha  eta} = \mathbf{A}^{\alpha} \mathbf{B}^{ eta}$ ? Can you express the test in coordinate-free language?

## Solution

Solution 3.11. In a basis $\mathbf{K}$ is represented by a matrix $\mathbf{K}^{a eta}$ . An obviously necessary and sufficient condition for $\mathbf{K}^{a eta}$ to be expressible as $\mathbf{A}^{\alpha}\mathbf{B}^{ eta}$ is that all columns of the matrix are proportional to each other.
In basis-free language, $\mathbf{K}$ can be written as
$$
\mathbf {K} = \mathbf {A} \otimes \mathbf {B}
$$
if and only if the vector $\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{v}$ (i.e. $\mathbf{W}^{\alpha} = \mathbf{K}^{a eta} \mathbf{v}_{ eta}$ ) is in the same direction for any choice of $\mathbf{v}$ . Proof: In some frame choose 4 basis vectors $\mathbf{e}^{0}, \mathbf{e}^{1}, \cdots$ to satisfy $\mathbf{e}^{\mu} \cdot \mathbf{e}_{
u} = \delta_{
u}^{\mu}$ . By linearity the direction of $\mathbf{W}$
is independent of any $\mathbf{v}$ if and only if it is independent of the 4 basis vectors $\mathbf{e}^{\mu}$ . Our condition then is
$$
\mathbf {K} \cdot \mathbf {e}^{0} = \lambda_{0} \mathbf {W}, \quad \mathbf {K} \cdot \mathbf {e}^{1} = \lambda_{1} \mathbf {W}, \dot s
$$
or
$$
\mathrm {K}^{\alpha 0} = \lambda_{0} \mathrm {W}^{\alpha}, \quad \mathrm {K}^{\alpha 1} = \lambda_{1} \mathrm {W}^{\alpha} \dot s .
$$
Our basis-free criterion then is equivalent to the columns being proportional.
origin.answer: Coordinate condition: All columns of the matrix representation $K^{a\\beta}$ are proportional to each other. Coordinate-free condition: For any arbitrary vector $\\mathbf{v}$, the resulting vector $\\mathbf{W} = \\mathbf{K} \\cdot \\mathbf{v}$ always points in a fixed direction (independent of the direction of $\\mathbf{v}$), which is equivalent to stating that the rank-2 tensor $\\mathbf{K}$ has rank 1.

Proof: In some frame choose 4 basis vectors $\mathbf{e}^{0}, \mathbf{e}^{1}, \cdots$ to satisfy $\mathbf{e}^{\mu} \cdot \mathbf{e}_{

u}^{\mu}$ . By linearity the direction of $\mathbf{W}$
